summary:
German Reunification was the process by which the German Democratic Republic (East Germany) acceded to the Federal Republic of Germany (West Germany) on 3 October 1990, less than a year after the [[event:2681|fall of the Berlin Wall]] in November 1989. The rapid collapse of East German communist rule created a political dynamic that made reunification virtually inevitable, though it required intensive international diplomacy to secure the consent of the four wartime powers — the United States, the Soviet Union, Britain, and France — who still retained formal occupation rights over Germany.

The international dimension was handled through the Two Plus Four Treaty, negotiated between the two German states and the four powers and signed in September 1990. The Soviet Union, under Gorbachev's leadership, agreed to accept a unified Germany in NATO, a concession of enormous strategic significance that would have been inconceivable even a year earlier. Chancellor Helmut Kohl of West Germany was the central political architect of reunification, pushing the process forward with remarkable speed.

The economic dimension of reunification proved more daunting than expected. East Germany's command economy had been deeply unproductive, its infrastructure was aging, and its enterprises were largely uncompetitive. The decision to convert East German marks to West German marks at a one-to-one rate — politically popular but economically controversial — contributed to rapid deindustrialization and high unemployment in the east. The cost of economic transformation ran to trillions of deutsche marks and created regional disparities that persisted for decades.

Reunification ended the post-World War II division of Europe and marked the peaceful conclusion of the [[event:113|Cold War]] in Central Europe. A unified Germany became the largest economy in Europe and a central pillar of the [[event:115|European Union]], fundamentally reshaping the continent's geopolitical landscape.
